new_ideas.title: Preserve council review and the charter's balance of powers
new_ideas.summary: Several elected officials and the council's own counsel argue that efficiency reforms should not shift authority from the City Council to the executive. They want to keep council review over the building code, DCAS property leases and dispositions, and major transportation projects, and to drop proposals (like letting DOB amend the building code without council approval or auto-sunsetting council voting authority) they say are unconstitutional or usurp council power. The framing: the charter must stay strong enough to check a future corrupt administration.

new_ideas.title: Condition outdoor dining licenses on paying a living wage
new_ideas.summary: One Fair Wage advocates and restaurant workers propose a charter amendment barring the city from issuing or renewing outdoor dining and outdoor-business licenses to businesses that do not pay their workers a living wage. They frame a living wage as a basic condition of doing business in the city and ask that the condition be placed before voters on the November ballot.

new_ideas.title: Name a commissioner-level owner of the city's cyber risk
new_ideas.summary: The city's former chief information security officer urges the charter to name a commissioner-level official who owns cyber risk, with a reporting line independent of IT delivery so the official can say no. He also wants public, auditable reporting on the city's cybersecurity posture.

new_ideas.title: Use the full two-year timeline instead of rushing ballot proposals
new_ideas.summary: Council officials urge the commission to use its full two-year timeline, deferring ballot proposals to 2027 rather than rushing them onto the November ballot. They want the commission to engage council subject-matter experts on concrete recommendations before issuing a final report, arguing there is nothing efficient about reaching the wrong policy outcome quickly.

new_ideas.title: Make the city liable for streets it knew were dangerous
new_ideas.summary: A street-safety advocate proposes making the city legally liable for damages on streets it knew were dangerous but failed to fix, creating a real enforcement incentive behind Vision Zero rather than an aspirational goal. The aim is to give the city a financial reason to act on known hazards.

new_ideas.title: Mandate that Correction protect the human dignity of everyone in its care
new_ideas.summary: The Vera Institute proposes adding a charter mandate that the Department of Correction is responsible for protecting the human dignity of everyone who works there or is in its care. The argument frames government efficiency as clarity of purpose and alignment of values, not just faster processes.

new_ideas.title: Reform the governance of student transportation
new_ideas.summary: Parent advocates want to change the governance of pupil transportation to align with the charter's user-focused design mandate, citing an Office of Pupil Transportation that spends roughly $2 billion a year producing two-hour routes for five-year-olds. They propose a ballot measure empowering a permanent commission of parents, educators, bus workers, students and disability advocates to make and implement pupil-transportation policy.

new_ideas.title: Fund safety-net and preventive care by tapping wealthy nonprofit hospitals
new_ideas.summary: A longtime public-health advocate argues the city must ensure preventive and primary care for people losing health insurance, calling it penny-wise and pound-foolish to skip that care. She proposes tapping the funds of wealthy voluntary nonprofit hospitals to support safety-net health-care providers in underserved communities.
